title: 7.4 practice
description: Practice what you learned in lesson 7.4!
topic_path: The Long Nineteenth Century (1750 to 1914 CE)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best explains why France is often considered the birthplace of modern nationalism?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Revolutionary leaders asserted that a nation’s power came from its people, not a monarch.
France was the first country to build a global empire through military conquest.
French monarchs encouraged pride in the kingdom to support their own power.
France was the first country in Europe to use industrial production for warfare.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes an unintended consequence of Napoleon Bonaparte’s use of nationalism in his empire?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It inspired nationalist movements in other parts of Europe demanding self-rule.
It caused France’s economy to collapse under the weight of war spending.
It led to an increase in loyalty to monarchs across Europe.
It weakened military recruitment as people resisted fighting for France.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best explains how Enlightenment ideas contributed to the rise of nationalism?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They encouraged unity around shared identities by promoting ideas like liberty and equality.
They called for absolute monarchies to maintain order in society.
They discouraged political participation and encouraged loyalty only to religion.
They focused mainly on economic growth and had little influence on political change.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What does the concept of “imagined communities” mean in the context of nationalism?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It describes how people feel connected to a nation even if they don’t personally know most members.
It means nations are only made up of small groups of people who personally know each other.
It suggests nationalism is based solely on shared language and religion.
It means that the idea of nations is not officially defined or recognized under international law.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How do the Olympic Games serve as a symbol of nationalism?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: By allowing countries to express pride in their national identity through athletic competition.
By eliminating national differences and encouraging athletes to compete under a single global flag.
By replacing traditional warfare with peaceful conflict between international organizations.
By limiting participation to countries that share similar cultural traditions and political systems.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why were nationalist movements in other parts of Europe more difficult to achieve than in France during the 19th century?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Many regions were divided by language, religion, and competing regional identities.
Most European rulers supported nationalist movements from the beginning.
France had the only military strong enough to suppress internal revolts.
Nationalism was illegal in most European countries during the 19th century.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes how the goals of the middle class and working class differed during the mid-19th-century revolutions?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The middle class focused on gaining political rights, while the working class pushed for better wages and working conditions.
The working class supported industrialization, while the middle class opposed it to protect small businesses.
The middle class demanded lower taxes, while the working class demanded to abolish all government institutions.
The middle class wanted the return of monarchies, while the working class advocated for a communal form of government.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did European monarchies use the differences between middle- and working-class to stay in power during the 19th-century revolutions?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They increased tensions by giving small political gains to the middle class for their support while stopping working-class demands.
They united the middle and working classes by promising new factory jobs, gaining enough support to retain power.
They took away rights from both the middle and working classes to treat them equally.
They fueled class divisions to justify using the army against the revolutionaries to eliminate the threat to their power.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best explains how European colonialism contributed to both the Taiping Rebellion in China and the Indian Uprising of 1857?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: European control weakened existing governments and created widespread resentment, fueling large-scale rebellions in both regions.
European countries sent troops to both regions to help support the rebellions against oppressive leaders.
Britain and France worked together to install new rulers in China and India, causing instability.
European missionaries directly led both rebellions in an effort to spread Christianity.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was a major global consequence of the failure of both the Taiping Rebellion and the Indian Uprising of 1857?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: European powers tightened control over China and India, expanding their global imperial reach.
China and India shifted away from resisting the empire and embraced Western reforms.
Britain and France reduced involvement in Asia and turned toward other regions.
The rebellions inspired immediate independence movements across other colonies.

assessment_items.question_content:
>The French people are henceforth part of a nation, united not by a king, but by their shared commitment to liberty, equality, and fraternity.

>—Jacques Roux, *Manifesto of the Enragés*, 1793


**How does this statement from the French Revolution illustrate the idea of nationalism?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It shows that nationalism is based on shared political ideals and identity, not loyalty to a monarch.
It suggests that loyalty to the monarchy is essential for national unity.
It indicates that nationalism depends on maintaining regional and local traditions.
It shows that the French people prioritized religious unity above all else.

assessment_items.question_content:
>A nation is not merely a common language, but a common character. The Jew is not of the same race as the German, and no amount of good will on his part can make him truly German... Only those who share in the soul and history of the nation can truly belong to it.

>—German nationalist Heinrich von Treitschke, 1879


**What was one negative consequence of the rise of nationalism in the 19th century, as reflected in the passage above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It led to the exclusion or persecution of groups who were not seen as part of the national identity.
It created rivalries among elites that weakened national governments from within.
It led some nations to reject industrialization as a foreign influence.
It caused rural populations to abandon national traditions in favor of urban culture.
